slides.title: What governor limits are
body: Salesforce caps how many resources any single transaction can use — queries, DML rows, CPU time, heap memory.
Real example: every multi-tenant SaaS has similar guardrails. AWS Lambda has memory and timeout limits. Cloudflare Workers cap CPU. The reason: one rogue tenant can't take down the whole platform.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce-imposed cap on resource usage per transaction (queries, DML, CPU, etc.).
One Apex execution unit — a single trigger fire, a single API call, a single batch chunk.

slides.title: The five limits you'll hit most
body: 100 SOQL queries · 150 DML statements · 50,000 rows queried · 10,000 rows DMLed · 10s sync CPU time.
Real example: 99% of governor failures involve crossing the 100-SOQL or 150-DML thresholds — almost always because a query or DML is sitting inside a for-loop.

slides.title: Why limits exist
body: Salesforce runs millions of orgs on shared hardware. Without caps, one badly-coded trigger could consume all the CPU on a server and degrade performance for thousands of customers.
Real example: this is the same reason Twitter rate-limits API calls per second per user. Multitenancy demands isolation.

vocabulary.def: Architecture where many customers share the same hardware/runtime. Drives the need for resource caps.

slides.title: The cardinal sin: SOQL inside a loop
body: for (Lead l : leads) { Account a = [SELECT ... WHERE Id = :l.AccountId]; } → 200 leads = 200 queries = blown 100-SOQL limit.
This is THE most common production failure pattern in junior Apex. The fix is bulkification.

slides.title: The broken pattern (don't write this)
slides.intro: Type this — it COMPILES, but blows the 100-SOQL limit on any trigger of 100+ records.
lineExplanations: SOQL inside the loop. Up to 200 queries. Past 100, runtime exception.
Even valid logic — but the structure dooms it.

slides.title: The bulkified fix
slides.intro: Type this. Collect Ids → ONE query → process from a Map.
lineExplanations: Set to collect parent Account Ids — Set dedupes automatically.
Skip nulls; otherwise add the Id.
ONE query (the only one) constructs a Map<Id, Account> directly from a SOQL result.
The IN :acctIds bind passes all collected Ids in a single query.

slides.title: DML inside a loop — same failure mode
body: Looping 'update someRecord;' 200 times = 200 DML statements. Past 150 = blown limit.
Fix: build a List of records to update in the loop. ONE 'update myList;' after the loop = 1 DML statement.

slides.title: Bulk DML pattern
slides.intro: Type this. One DML at the end, never inside the loop.
lineExplanations: Collect records that need an update.
Add to the to-update list — no DML yet.
ONE DML statement updates them all at once.

slides.title: Heap, CPU, and rows
body: Heap (memory): 6 MB sync, 12 MB async. Querying 1M records into a List eats heap fast.
CPU: 10 seconds sync. Tight nested loops over big collections will time out.

vocabulary.def: Total memory used by all variables in the transaction. Capped at 6MB sync / 12MB async.

slides.title: Limits.* methods
body: Apex exposes runtime: Limits.getQueries(), Limits.getDmlStatements(), Limits.getCpuTime() — and Limits.getLimitQueries() for the cap.
Real example: production-grade code logs Limits.getCpuTime() at key points for performance debugging when slow trigger fires.

slides.title: Inspecting limits at runtime
slides.intro: Type this. Helpful for debugging slow code.
lineExplanations: How many queries this transaction has used so far.
CPU time consumed (ms). Compare with Limits.getLimitCpuTime().

slides.front: What's the single most common reason Apex code fails in production?
slides.back: A SOQL query or DML statement inside a for-loop, hit by a 200-record trigger.
slides.detail: Bulkification (collect → query once → process) is the cure. Internalize this pattern; it shows up every day in real Apex work.

slides.question: Your trigger inserts a related record per Lead processed. The trigger fires on 200 leads. Which pattern is safe?
options: Loop the leads, insert one related record each time.
Build a List<RelatedRecord> in the loop, ONE 'insert listOfRelated;' after the loop.
Use a while-loop instead of a for-loop.
slides.explanation: One bulk DML after the loop. The first option = 200 DML statements, well past the 150 cap.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Why governor limits exist (multitenancy), the five you'll hit most, the bulkification pattern (collect → one query → one DML), and Limits.* introspection.
Next: APIs — making outbound HTTP calls and exposing your own Apex as REST endpoints.
